channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump’s Tenuous Relationship With NATO Allies
item.description: At this year’s NATO summit in Turkey, President Trump is arriving with his own priorities — and, as Tyler Pager, The Times’s White House correspondent, explains, they do not necessarily align with those of other NATO leaders.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager, Nour Idriss, Nikolay Nikolov, Stephanie Swart, Rafaela Balster and Ray Whitehouse

item.title: U.S. Loss Is a Gut Punch, but a Bright Future Remains for Soccer Here
item.description: While the team failed to meet the hopes of many fans, this World Cup highlighted the game’s decades-long climb into the national consciousness. What comes next?
item.dc:creator: David Waldstein
item.media:credit: Manu Fernandez/Associated Press
item.media:description: Folarin Balogun of the United States during the team’s 4-1 loss to Belgium in Seattle on Monday. The team was eliminated from the World Cup, but soccer stakeholders are upbeat about the sport’s future.

item.title: In CNN Interview, Woman Details Alleged Sexual Assault by Graham Platner
item.description: “This is something that I tried for many years to forget,” the woman, Jenny Racicot, said on CNN.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner has faced calls from Democrats to drop out of the Maine Senate race after an allegation of sexual assault by Jenny Racicot, a woman he once dated.

item.title: The World Cup Revived Seattle’s Downtown. Will the Energy Last?
item.description: The city struggled for years after the pandemic shutdown and the rush to remote work. But downtown has been full of soccer fans for the past month.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango and Mike Baker
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times
item.media:description: Seattle has used the World Cup as an opportunity to revitalize its downtown by drawing visitors to watch parties and celebrations before games. Local leaders have also tried to move chronically homeless people into shelters.

item.title: Trump Expected to Tell Turkey He Is Ready to Restore Access to F-35 Jets
item.description: The president, who is headed to a NATO summit in Ankara this week, had imposed the ban himself amid concerns that giving Turkey the jets could allow Russia to learn about their stealth technology.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and David E. Sanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: A model of an F-35 in the Oval Office. Turkey has been lobbying furiously for readmission to the fighter jet program for years.

item.title: Platner Faces Growing Democratic Calls to Drop Out
item.description: Even supporters like Representative Ro Khanna of California and Senator Elizabeth Warren of Massachusetts called on the Democratic nominee to exit the Maine Senate race after a report that he had sexually assaulted a woman.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies and Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Ro Khanna, a California Democrat who had been one of Graham Platner’s most vocal supporters, said on Monday that he was withdrawing his endorsement.

item.title: Trump Tries to Take Credit for Walmart Price Cuts of Beef and Other Products
item.description: The retailer said it will lower prices for beef, produce, drinks, pools, toys, grills and summer clothing.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Scott Olson/Getty Images
item.media:description: A Walmart store in Chicago.

item.title: Who Might Replace Graham Platner if He Drops Out of the Maine Senate Race?
item.description: Graham Platner can be replaced on the ballot if he withdraws in the next week. If he does, Maine Democrats would face an uncertain two-week race to choose a replacement.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner won the Democratic nomination for Senate in early June, but he could still be replaced on the ballot.

item.title: What Polls Say About the Fight for the Senate
item.description: Two of our political reporters dig into a revealing week of surveys.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times

item.title: Inside Trump’s Ideological Fight With the Smithsonian
item.description: Publicly and behind the scenes, the president continues to try to impose his own views of American history and culture, presenting an ongoing challenge to Lonnie Bunch, the institution’s leader.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.category: National Museum of African American History and Culture
item.media:credit: Lawren Simmons for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and his allies have criticized what they call “improper ideology” in the Smithsonian’s presentation of American history and culture.

item.title: Key Health Care Panel Is Again Blocked From Meeting Under RFK Jr.
item.description: The United States Preventive Services Task Force has been stymied under Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Astor
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. canceled meetings of the United States Preventive Services Task Force last July, last November and this March.

item.title: Platner’s campaign has been dogged by controversy from the beginning.
item.dc:creator: Felice Belman
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner, the Democratic Senate candidate in Maine, has had to navigate a series of controversies, including reports about his treatment of women and a tattoo that resembled a Nazi symbol.

item.title: Judge Appointed Last Year Is Overseeing Hearing in Killing of Charlie Kirk
item.description: Judge Tony Graf previously served as a prosecutor in Utah, including in the county where Mr. Kirk was shot and killed last year.
item.dc:creator: Madaleine Rubin
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Trent Nelson
item.media:description: Judge Tony Graf at court in April.

item.title: Platner’s Democratic Support Evaporates After Sexual Assault Allegation
item.description: Graham Platner, the Democratic Senate candidate in Maine, called the allegation false but said he would “reflect” on his political path forward, as many allies rescinded their endorsements.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner has until July 13 to withdraw from the Maine Senate race, and if he does, the state Democratic Party has until July 27 to replace him on the ticket, according to state law.

item.title: Four Dead, Including a Police Officer, in Ohio Shooting
item.description: Law enforcement officers were investigating a break-in and gunfire in Rittman. A suspect, his former partner and her teenage daughter were also dead.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.media:credit: WEWS
item.media:description: Four people, including a police officer, were fatally shot on Sunday night in Rittman, Ohio.

item.title: A fierce competition for seating and a few surprises in the courtroom.
item.dc:creator: Robert Draper
item.media:credit: Jim Urquhart/Reuters
item.media:description: People waiting in line on Monday to enter a courthouse in Provo, Utah, before a preliminary hearing for Tyler Robinson, the man accused of killing Charlie Kirk.

item.title: McConnell Has Been Hospitalized for 3 Weeks, and Aides Won’t Say Why
item.description: Senator Mitch McConnell was admitted to the hospital on June 14, and his office has provided few updates about his condition. Here’s what we know.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Mitch McConnell, 84, had in recent months used a wheelchair to travel from his office building to the Capitol, although days before his hospitalization he was able to walk on his own.

item.title: ‘Excited but Shocked’: U.S. Soccer Fans Struggle With Trump’s FIFA Call
item.description: Americans wrestled with definitions of fairness, after the president intervened in the World Cup to reinstate a star player.
item.dc:creator: Billy Witz
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times
item.media:description: Folarin Balogun, after the United States won its match against Australia last month.

item.title: Erika Kirk Attends Hearing for Man Accused of Killing Her Husband
item.description: Mrs. Kirk is at the center of conspiracy theories, atop a political organization in transition and facing a personal test.
item.dc:creator: Robert Draper
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Erika Kirk has endeavored to keep appearances to a minimum.

item.title: Michigan Senate Primary Gives Democrats Stark Choice Between Progressive and Moderate Candidate
item.description: The state’s important Senate primary just became a head-to-head battle between Abdul El-Sayed, an outspoken left-wing candidate, and Haley Stevens, a moderate backed by party leaders.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times; Nick Hagen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Haley Stevens of Michigan has an advertising edge in the state’s Democratic primary for Senate, but Dr. Abdul El-Sayed has led in polls.

item.title: Supreme Court Allows Texas Age-Verification Law for App Stores, for Now
item.description: The law would require Apple and Google to verify the age of app store users to give parents more control over the content their children download.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Texas is one of 20 states that has passed or considered similar age-verification laws for electronic devices.

item.title: Trump Defends Asking FIFA to Review Folarin Balogun’s World Cup Ban
item.description: The president said he called the head of FIFA but “didn’t tell him what to do.” The overturning of the ban has roiled soccer and brought renewed scrutiny to the relationship between the two leaders.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green and Tariq Panja
item.category: United States Men's National Soccer Team
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump received the first FIFA Peace Prize from the head of the soccer governing body, Gianni Infantino, in December.

item.title: President Rings Stock Market Opening Bell to Celebrate Trump Accounts
item.description: The government-funded savings accounts for children became available on Saturday.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni
item.media:credit: Mandel Ngan, via Getty Images

item.title: Efforts to Help Smokers Quit Stall Under Trump
item.description: The C.D.C.’s tobacco control office has been shut for more than a year. After its most prominent antismoking campaign went off the air, calls to 1-800-QUIT-NOW lines have plummeted.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

item.title: Key Moments in the Case of Charlie Kirk’s Assassination
item.description: The case against the defendant, Tyler Robinson, has played out since last September.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Charlie Kirk has been cast as a martyr by conservatives.

item.title: Who Was Charlie Kirk?
item.description: Mr. Kirk, an influential right-wing activist, has become a modern-day martyr for conservative evangelical Christians.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times

item.title: In Trump’s Second Midterm, Democrats Are Looking for Fighters
item.description: Attitudes in the party have shifted, Democrats say, with a growing appetite for outsider candidates vowing to overhaul the system. Some worry that the trend could be costly in November.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Madison Swart for The New York Times
item.media:description: Darializa Avila Chevalier, left, and Claire Valdez, holding the U.A.W. sign, are on track to double the number of Democratic Socialists of America members in the House. They flank Brad Lander, a Democrat, who also won his primary.

item.title: New Videos of Charlie Kirk’s Killing Emerge in Court, but Few Can Watch
item.description: On Day 1 of what is expected to be a weeklong hearing, prosecutors played videos of the shooting for a judge, but they were graphic and kept from public view. Erika Kirk and Donald Trump Jr. were in court.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy, Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs and Robert Draper
item.media:credit: Kevin Lamarque/Reuters

item.title: Research Universities Are Admitting Fewer Ph.D.s, a Bad Sign for Science
item.description: Some of the nation’s top research universities are shrinking doctoral programs because of uncertain federal funding.
item.dc:creator: Vimal Patel
item.media:credit: Cody O'Loughlin for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge, Mass.

item.title: What Capitol Hill Interns Are Posting on Social Media This Summer
item.description: Congressional interns, usually seen but not heard, are taking to social media with viral trends and pithy posts about their outfits, lifestyles and jobs.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Diaz

item.title: For the Young (and Old), World Cup Memories Last a Lifetime
item.description: A Times reporter reflects on her childhood experience at the World Cup and how attending a game can shape the children of today.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: A young Brazil fan at their game against Scotland.

item.title: Emil Bove Defended Trump in Court. Then Trump Made Him a Judge.
item.description: President Trump is appointing his former personal lawyers to judgeships, part of a larger effort to put his stamp on the federal courts.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.category: Eric Adams Federal Corruption Case (24 CR 556)
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Emil Bove III, right, represented Donald J. Trump during his trial in Manhattan related to hush money payments in 2024.
